## Results

This section splits the treatment's effect into the part that flows through the mediator and the part that does not. The direct effect (NDE) is what treatment does on its own, the indirect effect (NIE) is what it does by moving the mediator, and the total effect is the two added together (NDE + NIE).

The mediation quantities are computed by counterfactual simulation from the
posterior g-formula. They are **not** products of coefficients, and they are
**not identified natural effects**. Two structural obstacles block a natural
NDE/NIE interpretation, and neither is removed by more data. First, every
mediator→outcome path is confounded by latent general ability (and, at a single
wave, by shared timing), so the split rests on an unverifiable
no-unmeasured-confounding assumption. Second — and independently — intervention
dose (sessions attended, `IS` in the DAG) is a **treatment-induced
(exposure-induced) mediator–outcome confounder**: it is a descendant of the
randomised group and points into both the mediator and word reading, so natural
direct/indirect effects are **not identified even by randomisation** and are
**not repaired by measuring and adjusting dose**, because dose is itself a
descendant of the exposure (VanderWeele, Vansteelandt & Robins 2014, DOI
[10.1097/EDE.0000000000000034](https://doi.org/10.1097/EDE.0000000000000034)).
Read these outputs as **model-based g-formula decompositions under the stated
(cross-world) assumptions, not identified natural effects**.

An **interventional** (rather than natural) mediation estimand — fitted for the
letter-sound route as `lrp-rli-med-078` — addresses the **second** obstacle only.
It invokes no cross-world quantity, so it is not defeated by the exposure-induced
confounder. It does **not** remove the first: identification of stochastic
interventional (in)direct effects still requires no unmeasured mediator–outcome
confounding (Hejazi, Rudolph, van der Laan & Díaz 2022, assumption A5, DOI
[10.1093/biostatistics/kxac002](https://doi.org/10.1093/biostatistics/kxac002)),
which latent general ability violates here, and their positivity requirement (A3)
and the absence of temporal ordering between mediator and outcome both remain
binding. The interventional estimand is therefore a **weaker-assumption target,
not an identified one**. The model-specific assumptions section states the
design-specific version.

_How to read the mediation table:_ each row is one decomposition quantity. `NDE` is the direct effect (treatment acting on its own), `NIE` is the indirect effect (treatment acting through the mediator), and `total` is their sum. The `words_*` columns give the posterior median and 95% credible interval in words; `prob_pos` is the probability the effect exceeds zero. `proportion_mediated` is the share of the total that runs through the mediator, and is unstable whenever the total can cross zero.

```{python}
# | echo: false
mediation_summary = _csv("mediation_summary.csv")
mediation_summary if mediation_summary is not None else "No mediation summary."
```

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | output: asis
# Off-floor OUTCOME (#228 item 12, e.g. nonword N): the effects are off-floor risk
# differences, so the words_* columns equal the prob_* columns (n_trials_W = 1).
_off = (
    mediation_summary is not None
    and "off_floor" in mediation_summary.columns
    and bool(mediation_summary["off_floor"].iloc[0])
)
if _off:
    print(
        '::: {.callout-important title="Off-floor outcome — effects are risk differences"}\n'
        "The outcome here is the **off-floor indicator** (post > 0), so NDE / NIE / total "
        "are **off-floor risk differences** — changes in the probability of coming off the "
        "floor attributable to the direct vs the through-mediator path — not item counts. "
        "The `words_*` columns therefore equal the probability-scale columns.\n:::\n"
    )
```

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | output: asis
if mediation_summary is not None:
    _m = mediation_summary.set_index("quantity")
    _total_name = "total" if "total" in _m.index else None
    _indirect_name = next(
        (n for n in ("NIE_joint", "NIE", "IIE") if n in _m.index), None
    )
    if _total_name and _indirect_name:
        _tot = _m.loc[_total_name]
        _ind = _m.loc[_indirect_name]
        print(
            f"**Total effect:** {_tot.words_median:+.1f} words "
            f"(90% CrI {_tot.words_lo90:+.1f} to {_tot.words_hi90:+.1f}; "
            f"95% {_tot.words_lo:+.1f} to {_tot.words_hi:+.1f}); "
            f"P(> 0) = {_tot.prob_pos:.3f}.\n\n"
            f"**Indirect path (`{_indirect_name}`):** {_ind.words_median:+.1f} words "
            f"(90% CrI {_ind.words_lo90:+.1f} to {_ind.words_hi90:+.1f}; "
            f"95% {_ind.words_lo:+.1f} to {_ind.words_hi:+.1f}); "
            f"P(> 0) = {_ind.prob_pos:.3f}."
        )
    if "proportion_mediated" in _m.index:
        _p = _m.loc["proportion_mediated"]
        print(
            f"\n\n**Proportion mediated/share:** median {_p.prob_median:.2f} "
            f"(90% CrI {_p.prob_lo90:.2f} to {_p.prob_hi90:.2f}; "
            f"95% {_p.prob_lo:.2f} to {_p.prob_hi:.2f}). This ratio is "
            "unstable when the total effect can cross zero; lead with Total, "
            "NDE, and NIE intervals."
        )
```

### Temporal-Ordering Sensitivity

_How to read this table:_ it re-runs the same NDE / NIE / total decomposition (same columns as above) but with the mediator measured before the outcome, so you can check whether the split points the same way when temporal order is enforced. Compare the direction and rough size against the main table rather than reading its numbers as the headline estimate.

```{python}
# | echo: false
mediation_summary_t3 = _csv("mediation_summary_t3.csv")
mediation_summary_t3 if mediation_summary_t3 is not None else "No t3 sensitivity table."
```

The t3 sensitivity puts the mediator before the outcome in time, but the later
contrast is no longer the clean randomised phase-0 contrast. Use it as a direction
check, not as a replacement estimand.

### Unmeasured mediator–outcome confounding sensitivity

The no-unmeasured-mediator–outcome-confounding assumption above is the binding one; this quantifies how fragile the indirect effect is to violating it. We sweep a bias δ off the mediator→outcome coefficient (`b_M`) — the share of the fitted mediator→reading association one attributes to an unmeasured confounder — and re-run the same g-formula, reporting the **tipping point**: the δ at which the indirect effect's credible interval first includes zero, expressed as a fraction of the fitted coefficient (a Bayesian E-value analogue). Larger ⇒ more robust; it bounds robustness, it does not prove absence of confounding.

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | output: asis
_sens = _csv("mediation_sensitivity_summary.csv")
if _sens is not None:
    _s = _sens.iloc[0]
    _robust = bool(_s.get("robust_over_full_sweep", False))
    if bool(_s.get("already_null_at_zero", False)):
        _msg = (
            "The indirect effect is not credibly non-zero at δ = 0, so there is no "
            "indirect effect to explain away — the sensitivity question does not apply here."
        )
    else:
        _msg = (
            (
                "The indirect effect's interval excludes zero across the entire sweep: "
                "no single unmeasured mediator–outcome confounder in this range nulls it."
            )
            if _robust
            else (
                f"**Tipping point:** an unmeasured mediator–outcome confounder inducing "
                f"a spurious mediator→reading association of δ* ≈ {_s.tipping_delta:.2f} "
                f"on the logit scale — about **{_s.tipping_frac_of_bM:.0%}** of the "
                f"fitted mediator→outcome coefficient — would be enough to bring the "
                f"indirect effect's interval to zero."
            )
        )
    print(_msg)
else:
    print("No mediation sensitivity summary.")
```

```{python}
# | echo: false
_csv("mediation_sensitivity.csv")
```

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | output: asis
_is_cal = _csv("mediation_is_calibration.csv")
if _is_cal is not None:
    _c = _is_cal.iloc[0]
    print("### Intervention-session named-confounder calibration\n")
    if str(_c.get("status", "")) != "ok":
        print(f"The IS calibration is not available for this fit: {_c.get('reason', 'required source artefacts are missing')}.\n")
    else:
        print(f"**Computed readout.** {_c.sentence}\n")
        _tip = "not finite" if pd.isna(_c.tipping_delta) else f"{_c.tipping_delta:.2f}"
        print(
            "| Quantity | Calibration value |\n"
            "|---|---:|\n"
            f"| Fitted `IS → L` slope (standardised calibration scale) | {_c.fitted_mediator_dose_slope:+.2f} |\n"
            f"| Fitted/observed `IS → outcome` slope (outcome logit) | {_c.fitted_outcome_dose_slope:+.2f} |\n"
            f"| IS point on the δ surface | {_c.delta_is_point:.2f} |\n"
            f"| 90% endpoint scenario band | {_c.delta_is_scenario_lo90:.2f} to {_c.delta_is_scenario_hi90:.2f} |\n"
            f"| NIE tipping δ* | {_tip} |\n"
            f"| NIE at the IS point ({_c.response_scale}) | {_c.nie_response_at_is:+.2f} ({_c.nie_response_lo_at_is:+.2f} to {_c.nie_response_hi_at_is:+.2f}) |\n"
        )
        print("This is a **scenario calibration, not an adjustment for sessions and not an identified causal effect**. It uses the single-omitted-variable approximation $\delta_{IS} \approx |\beta_{IS\to L}\,\beta_{IS\to Y}|$ after putting both dose associations on the mediation sample's one-standard-deviation session scale. The band is the envelope of separate 90% marginal slope endpoints plus the observed-data cross-check; it is not a joint credible interval, and treating the whole `IS → outcome` association as confounding may overstate bias because that association can include a genuine `IS → L → outcome` path.\n")
        if str(_c.get("off_floor", "")).strip().lower() in {"true", "1"}:
            print("For the off-floor outcome, δ is necessarily calibrated as a shift in the Bernoulli outcome's **log-odds coefficient**; there is no constant conversion from log odds to a risk difference. The sensitivity sweep instead re-runs the Bernoulli g-formula at that δ and the table reports the resulting NIE on the **off-floor risk-difference** scale.\n")
```
